channel.title: NLPenguin on NLPenguin
channel.description: Recent content in NLPenguin on NLPenguin

item.title: Notes from EMNLP 2018
item.description:
I’ve returned from EMNLP, my first big NLP conference, full of ideas, impressions and inspiration. I went there to present my paper on bipolar disorder prediction on Reddit at WASSA and, of course, to learn as much as I can! You can find my paper here. And yes, my talk went great, thanks for asking.

Here I’d like to share my notes from all the talks I’ve attended. These were initially meant just for me, but when I started transcripting them on a computer, I got an idea to share them as well.

Table of Contents:

  1. Day 1

    • Keynote by Julia Hirschberg

    • Session 1: Social Applications 1

    • Session 2: Social Media

    • Session 3: Machine Learning

    • Session 4: Language Models

  2. Day 2

    • Session 5: Machine Learning 2

    • Session 7: Social Application 2

    • Keynote by Gideon Mann

    • Session 8: Text Categorization

DAY 1

Julia Hirschberg Keynote: “Truth or Lie? Spoken Indicators of Deception in Speech”
notes
  • deceptive speech – deliberate choice to mislead
  • criminals are better than police, judges and similar in detecting it
  • there are acoustic-prosodic and lexical cues
  • system should take individual differences into account: gender, ethinicity…
  • existing dataset: Columbia SRI Colorado Deception corpus (7h of speech, 32 subjects, 70% acc)
  • created a cross-lingual dataset: 340 subjects, 122h of speech, native english speakers questioning native chinese speakers who know english (and vice versa)
  • features: n-grams, LIWC, word embeddings, speech-based (openSMILE, ISO9), gender, native language, NEO-FFIs personality scores, syntactic features
  • Methods: Random Forest, SVM, DL – BiLSTM-lexical + DNN-speech yielding 0.65 F1
  • Best model: Multi-Task Learning with personality traits 74.4 F1
  • Significant differences in deceptive speech: Men are loud with higher pich when lying; Woman are just loud, but have jitter when telling the truth
  • Human accuracy: 56.76%

Session 1: Social Applications 1

Privacy-preserving Neural Representations of Text
Coavoux, Narayan, and Cohen
  • explicit private information can be preprocessed, but implicit (age, authorship) can’t
  • Can attacker guess about input text based on hidden representation?
  • Target private variables: age, gender, named entities that occur in the text
  • attacker eavesdrops while learning the model – attacker beats the baseline for age and gender just based on hidden r
  • defence: double objective (loss_clf = -logP(y|x) - loss_attacker) or adversarial generation (LM)
  • conclusion: latent representations contain a signal for private information
  • code on github
Adversarial Removal of Demographic Attributes from Text Data
Elazar and Goldberg
  • while learning DeepEmoji attacker eavesdrops –> hich leakage
  • Actively reduce leakage with adversarial setup (Ganing and Lempitsy, 2015) –> remove stuff from representation, but still some leakage
  • Their method: use more layers for adversarial reversal layer; bigger \lambda to reverse with; > 1 adversaries –> they learned diff features
  • still not perfect!
DeClarE: Debunking Fake News and False Claims using Evidence-Aware Deep Learning
Popat, Mukherjee, Yates, and Weikum
  • prior work: supervised learning (doesn’t use external knowledge) & external sources (require feature modeling and lexicons). Why people don’t like feature modeling any more? :)
  • overcame flaws with: end-to-end NN that takes the external resources into account without human intervention
  • Approach:
    • external evidence – search engine
    • language style, semantics – BiLSTM
    • interaction claim-external – Attention
    • credibility score based on all of that
  • beat the baseline on 4 datasets
  • analysis showed clear clusters of Fake and Trusted websites (CNN…)
It’s going to be okay: Measuring Access to Support in Online Communities
Wang and Jurgens
  • minimal identity cues are important – username that reveals gender or interests (catlady22, pizzamaniac…)
  • support is more than encouragement (14 strategies: validation, advice…)
  • dataset: post-reply from Reddit, Wiki, Stack Exchange
  • goal: rate the reply as: support, polite, agree, offensive
  • most important features are from LIWC –> support in theory
  • constructed massive gender-performance DS from usernames
  • Who recieves support? – woman get more support, but also more disparagement
  • code and data on github
Detecting Gang-Involved Escalation on Social Media Using Context

Chang et al.

Session 2: Social Media

Joint Learning for Emotion Classification and Emotion Cause Detection
Chen, Hou, Cheng, and Li
  • two tasks: (1) emotion classification and (2) emotion cause detection
  • they argue it’s not hard to label cause while you are labeling for emotions
  • joint encoder & linear decoder
  • joint learning improves F1 (is it significant? –> check paper)
Exploring Optimism and Pessimism in Twitter Using Deep Learning
Caragea, Dinu, and Dumitru
  • they argue there is a difference between sentiment and optimisim/pessimism
  • Dataset: 10k tweets labeled between -3 (most pessimistic) to 3 (most optimistic)
  • when 0 is treshold –> 80% Acc; when (-1) and 1 are –> 90% Acc
  • deep learning architecture
  • different from sentiment: when trained on sentiment dataset, they get poor results. Q: They trained only Stanford analyzer, what when you train a NN like for the main task?
  • verb tense analysis
Predicting News Headline Popularity with Syntactic and Semantic Knowledge Using Multi-Task Learning
Lamprinidis, Hardt, and Hovy
  • why do you click on a Facebook post?
  • modeling reader’s knowledge – lexical, syntactic, topical
  • Dataset: 82k headlines with click numbers. Then make it a binary classification >50% median are often clicked ones, <50% rarely clicked. Q: Seems kinda random.
  • Auxiliary task: POS tagging
  • GRU-MTL with pretrained embeddings yields better performance than the task alone
  • Q: Why does POS matter for this? How to know which aux task will help? Also, position of a post, image of a post?
Hybrid Neural Attention for Agreement/Disagreement Inference in Online Debates
Chen, Du, Bing, and Xu
  • does a reply agree or disagree with the comment?
  • two factors:
    • self-attention –> focuses on important parts inside a comment
    • cross-comment-attention –> reply focused
  • connect both with something, idk what because they show huge NN for 2 sec :(
  • Attention visualisation & Cross Matrix Product Visualisation –> TODO: Learn how to do that!
Increasing In-Class Similarity by Retrofitting Embeddings with Demographic Information
Hovy and Fornaciari
  • Homophily?
  • retrofitting – moving the points of distinct classes away from each other (x’, y’) = a(x, y) + b(mean)
  • for test data use translation matrix calculated from the train set
  • benefit in gender and age performance
  • Q: Not different than just learning the embeddings? If you train your embeddings, you’re sepperating classes?

Session 3: Machine Learning

Self-Governing Neural Networks for On-Device Short Text Classification
Ravi and Kozareva
  • the only presentation I’ve seen with multiple people :)
  • TFLite –> it’s a lite Tensorflow
  • SGNN - projection based NN –> get rid of the embedding layer and use locality sensitive hashing as feature extractor
  • on 42 dialog acts they got SOTA. Q: What if they trained that model without memory/performance restrictions? SOTA on a bunch of other things?
Supervised Domain Enablement Attention for Personalized Domain Classification
Kim and Kim
  • didn’t catch much
A Deep Neural Network Sentence Level Classification Method with Context Information
Song, Petrak, and Roberts
  • sucidal classification in medical data –> from a text, predict if a specific sentence expresses suicidal thoughts.
  • BiLSTM + CNN on focus sentences
  • provide right + left context – FOFE (fixed-size ordinally forgetting encoding)
  • reports significant improvements
  • takeaway: context is important (who’d say right :)

Session 4: Language Models

Efficient Contextualized Representation: Language Model Pruning for Sequence Labeling
Liu et al.
  • ELMo is great, but it contains sufficent information –> prune it w.r.t. the task you’re solving
  • dense connectivity – alows better layer selection via layer-wise dropout
  • sequence labeling with contextualized representations (PPL?)
  • nice experiments, results comparable with SOTA
  • size reduced x10, speed goes up x5-6, Acc down by just 1%
  • integrated & pruned plug-and-play model

DAY 2

Session 5: Machine Learning 2

Deriving Machine Attention from Human Rationales
Bao, Chang, Yu, and Barzilay
  • they wanna train NN with less data
  • rationales – part of a sentence that directs sentiment –> use them to direct attention
  • Rationale2Attention – transformable from task to task
    • train R2A on source task
    • use R2A to generate attention for target task
    • train a target classifier with R2A attention
  • domain-ivariant encoder –> Attention Generator (shared BiLSTM, att.sumarizer) –> MTL (shared BiLSTM, task-specific Attention and MLP)
  • 200 examples + rationales gets the same results as 3100 examples –> cheaper annotation
  • code on github
Semi-Supervised Sequence Modeling with Cross-View Training
Clark, Luong, Manning, and Le
  • revisit self-training (older method) with NN
  • Blum 1998. idea: 2 models teach each other
  • alternate labeled and unlabeled batches –> how to use it?
  • Cross-View Training – not just exchange predictions like Blum, but use NN shared parameters
    • 2 models (auxiliary models) + Main Predictor
    • Forward LSTM –> Forward prediction; Backward LSTM –> Backward prediction
    • Main Predictor teaches Forward
    • 4 aux tasks
  • CVT + MTL on 1B unlabeled data create all labels for all tasks on one example
  • 4 sequence tagging, dependency parsing, machine translation
  • significant improvement for CVT + MTL (over ELMo too)
[TACL] Comparing Bayesian Models of Annotation
Paun et al.
  • probabilistic models of annotation are better than majority vote and similar

Session 7: Social Applications 2

Gideon Mann Keynote: Understanding the News that Moves Markets
notes
  • we live in a scary world where one headline crashes the stock market
  • some systems react in miliseconds after some article is published (buy/sell stocks)

Session 8: Text Categorization

Zero-shot User Intent Detection via Capsule Neural Networks
Xia et al.
  • how to handle emerging intents?
  • would be nice if he explained what zero-shot detection and capsules are :D (I managed to figure out for zero-shot, but capsule…note to myself: never assume an audiance knows something)
Hierarchical Neural Networks for Sequential Sentence Classification in Medical Scientific Abstracts
Jin and Szolovits
  • sequentialy label each sentence in a paragraph (abstract): Background, Methods, Results…
  • contextual sentence features have not been looked into. What is done is CRF which considers context labels
  • they pre-trained word embeddings on scientific papers
  • architecture: embedding layer –> LSTM on sentence level –> LSTM on paragraph level
  • new sota for the task
  • ablation study –> context is the most important
Investigating Capsule Networks with Dynamic Routing for Text Classification
Yang et al.
  • kinda figured out what a capsule is. Standard CNN kernel output is a scalar, while a capsule outputs a vector (with orientation, length and everything) – somehow this is useful :)
  • they propose a capsule net for text classification
Topic Memory Networks for Short Text Classification
Zeng et al.
  • topic model – each topic is a distribution of words
  • Neural topic model – generative model
  • put topic memory mechanism on top of NTM, then classifier
  • topic coherence
Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Multi-Label Learning for Structured Label Spaces
Rios and Kavuluru
  • medical coding – one EMR has 15 codes, 1000 code combinations to chose from
  • graph convolution label embeddings? GCNN over descriptors hierarchy –> concat word vectors and final GCNN layer
  • 2 components: text –> CNN & descriptors –> GCNN
  • conect them with label-wise attention (otherwise, finding relevant subtext would be finding a needle in a haystack)
  • they test few label groups: frequent (> 5 ocurrances), few-shot (>= 1), zero-shot (=0)
  • train jointly, evaluate seperately R@k
  • TODO: read the paper

Sunday

Session 9: Sentiment 1

Sentiment Classification towards Question-Answering with Hierarchical Matching Network
Shen et al.
  • BiLSTM to get encoding of a Question and an Answer
  • row-wise Answer-to-Question attention & Question-to-Answer
  • Bidirectional Matching Vectors + self-attention
  • sentence segmentation helps to extract relevant subtext
  • Q&A attentions contribute in both ways
  • dataset on github
Cross-topic Argument Mining from Heterogeneous Sources
Stab et al.
  • information seeking perspective –> extract pros and cons
  • apply model to heterogeneous texts (8 topics, 25k docs, blogs/news/social media etc.)
  • Labels: pro, con, no-argument
  • 200 random sentences for each topic for calibration (0.72 Kappa) –> 25k sentences with crowdsourcing annotation
  • Bi Contextual LSTM (Gogh et al, 2017)
  • Tried transfer learning & MTL (topic_main & topic_otherdataset)
  • train on 7, test on 1 topic
  • the more you add, the more you get
  • online demo (couldn’t find it)
Summarizing Opinions: Aspect Extraction Meets Sentiment Prediction and They Are Both Weekly Supervised
Angelidis and Lapata
  • I like the title
  • extract small number of reviews of a product with different aspects and summarize it (and add sentiment)
  • not enought data –> inject prior knowledge with seed words
  • Multi-Seed Aspect Extractor (MATE) – init seed matrices for each aspect (image, sound of a TV…)
  • add MTL
  • Sentiment prediction: multiple instance learning network
  • dataset: aspect labels, opinion salience labels, find summaries
  • TODO: read the paper

Session 9: Machine Learning 3

Phrase-level Self-Attention Networks for Universal Sentence Encoding
Wu, Wang, Liu, and Ma
  • pre-trained embedding options:
    • LM – ELMo, BERT
    • supervised learning – their choice –> smaller model, faster training
    • incorporate syntax into fully-attention network – compose sentence representation on phrased from the dependency tree –> phrase-level self-attention
BanditSum: Extractive Summarization as a Contextual Bandit
Dong et al.
  • how to select the right sentence to include in a summary?
  • Problem: from continuous NN to discrete (N sent, ROUGE)
  • Sequential Tagging with Reinforcment Learning (proposed before) –> probl: stop sampling once M sentences are selected
  • Approach: treat extractive summarization as contextual Bandit (Context-Arm-Reward)
    • smaller search space
    • no heuristics needed
    • SOTA

######

######

######

######
